questions.question: Boy A and Boy B have equal mass. They climb to the same height, but Boy A reaches the top in a shorter time. How does their power compare?
options: Boy A has more power than Boy B
Boy B has more power than Boy A
They have equal power because their masses are equal
They have equal power because they climbed the same distance
questions.explanation:
Both do the same work because they have the same mass and height. However, Boy A is more powerful because he performs that work more quickly. 

**Common mistake:** Equating power only to the amount of work done without considering the time duration.

questions.question: An electric motor in a washing machine has a power output of $1.0\text{ kW}$. Calculate the work done by the motor in half an hour ($30\text{ minutes}$).
questions.explanation:
$P = 1000\text{ W}$; $t = 30 \times 60 = 1800\text{ s}$. $Work = P \times t = 1000 \times 1800 = 1800000\text{ J} = 1.8\text{ MJ}$. 

**Common mistake:** Failing to convert half an hour into seconds before calculating.

questions.question: Two electric kettles contain the same amount of water. Kettle X is rated at $500\text{ W}$ and Kettle Y is rated at $1000\text{ W}$. How does the time taken to boil the water compare?
options: Kettle Y will take half the time of Kettle X
Kettle X will take half the time of Kettle Y
They will take the same amount of time
$Kettle Y will take four \times as long as Kettle X$
questions.explanation:
A higher power rating means energy is transferred faster. Since Kettle Y has double the power of Kettle X, it can transfer the same required boiling energy in half the time. 

**Common mistake:** Reversing the logic and thinking higher power takes longer.

questions.question: A student lifts a $2.0\text{ kg}$ mass vertically through a height of $5.0\text{ m}$ in $4.0\text{ s}$. Calculate the power used against gravity. (Take $g = 10\text{ N/kg}$)
questions.explanation:
Work $W = mgh = 2.0 \times 10 \times 5.0 = 100\text{ J}$. Power $P = W / t = 100 / 4.0 = 25\text{ W}$. 

**Common mistake:** Forgetting to multiply by $g$ ($10$) to get the weight, leading to $2.5\text{ W}$.

questions.question: An electric hoist has a total power input of $500\text{ W}$ and an efficiency of $60\%$. Calculate the useful power output.
questions.explanation:
Useful Power $= \text{Efficiency} \times \text{Total Input} = 0.60 \times 500 = 300\text{ W}$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing input by efficiency ($833$) or calculating the wasted power ($200$).
